question: Explain what garbage collection is in Java and why it is important.
expectedPoints: Garbage collection is the automatic process of reclaiming memory by destroying objects that are no longer reachable or referenced by the application.
It prevents memory leaks by ensuring that unused objects do not occupy memory indefinitely.
It simplifies memory management for developers, reducing the risk of manual memory management errors like dangling pointers or double-free issues.

question: Describe how an object becomes eligible for garbage collection in Java and explain the role of the finalize() method in this process.
expectedPoints: An object becomes eligible for garbage collection when there are no more references pointing to it, meaning it is unreachable.
Common ways to make an object eligible include nullifying a reference, reassigning a reference, or having an object created inside a method (local variable) after the method ends.
The finalize() method is called by the garbage collector just before destroying the object, allowing it to perform cleanup operations; however, it is deprecated and its execution is not guaranteed.

question: Compare and contrast the Mark-and-Sweep garbage collection algorithm with the Generational Garbage Collection approach used in the Java HotSpot VM. Discuss the advantages and potential drawbacks of each.
expectedPoints: Mark-and-Sweep is a two-phase algorithm: it first marks all reachable objects from the root set, then sweeps through the heap to deallocate unmarked (unreachable) objects. A drawback is that it can cause heap fragmentation.

Generational Garbage Collection divides the heap into generations (Young and Old) based on object lifespan. Most objects die young, so frequent, minor collections in the Young Generation (Eden, S0, S1) are efficient using a copying collector.

The Generational approach reduces pause times and improves throughput by focusing effort on the area where garbage is most likely to be found. Its complexity is a potential drawback, but it is highly optimized for real-world application behavior.
